data.title: Income Investors Should Know That Aker ASA (OB:AKER) Goes Ex-Dividend Soon
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Aker ASA (OB:AKER) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next three days. The ex-dividend date is usually set to be two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date on which you must be present on the company's books as a shareholder in order to receive the dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Thus, you can purchase Aker's shares before the 23rd of April in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 5th of May.

The company's upcoming dividend is kr029.00 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of kr53.00 per share to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Aker has a trailing yield of 5.4% on the current share price of kr01026.00. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Aker's dividend is reliable and sustainable. As a result, readers should always check whether Aker has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. Aker paid out more than half (53%) of its earnings last year, which is a regular payout ratio for most companies. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Over the last year, it paid out dividends equivalent to 272% of what it generated in free cash flow, a disturbingly high percentage. Unless there were something in the business we're not grasping, this could signal a risk that the dividend may have to be cut in the future.

Aker paid out less in dividends than it reported in profits, but unfortunately it didn't generate enough cash to cover the dividend. Cash is king, as they say, and were Aker to repeatedly pay dividends that aren't well covered by cashflow, we would consider this a warning sign.

View our latest analysis for Aker

Click here to see how much of its profit Aker paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. That's why it's comforting to see Aker's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 35% per annum for the past five years. Earnings have been growing quickly, but we're concerned dividend payments consumed most of the company's cash flow over the past year.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. Aker has delivered 19% dividend growth per year on average over the past 10 years. It's great to see earnings per share growing rapidly over several years, and dividends per share growing right along with it.

Final Takeaway

Is Aker an attractive dividend stock, or better left on the shelf? It's good to see that earnings per share are growing and that the company's payout ratio is within a normal range for most businesses. However we're somewhat concerned that it paid out 272% of its cashflow, which is uncomfortably high. Overall, it's hard to get excited about Aker from a dividend perspective.

However if you're still interested in Aker as a potential investment, you should definitely consider some of the risks involved with Aker. For instance, we've identified 4 warning signs for Aker (2 are a bit concerning) you should be aware of.

data.title: Only Three Days Left To Cash In On PPHE Hotel Group's (LON:PPH) Dividend
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see PPHE Hotel Group Limited (LON:PPH) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 3 days. The ex-dividend date is commonly two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. The ex-dividend date is an important date to be aware of as any purchase of the stock made on or after this date might mean a late settlement that doesn't show on the record date. Meaning, you will need to purchase PPHE Hotel Group's shares before the 23rd of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 29th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be UK£0.22 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of UK£0.39 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that PPHE Hotel Group has a trailing yield of 2.2% on the current share price of UK£17.50. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. PPHE Hotel Group distributed an unsustainably high 124% of its profit as dividends to shareholders last year. Without more sustainable payment behaviour, the dividend looks precarious. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Fortunately, it paid out only 33% of its free cash flow in the past year.

It's disappointing to see that the dividend was not covered by profits, but cash is more important from a dividend sustainability perspective, and PPHE Hotel Group fortunately did generate enough cash to fund its dividend. If executives were to continue paying more in dividends than the company reported in profits, we'd view this as a warning sign. Very few companies are able to sustainably pay dividends larger than their reported earnings.

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. It's encouraging to see PPHE Hotel Group has grown its earnings rapidly, up 66% a year for the past five years.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. PPHE Hotel Group has delivered an average of 6.9% per year annual increase in its dividend, based on the past 10 years of dividend payments. It's encouraging to see the company lifting dividends while earnings are growing, suggesting at least some corporate interest in rewarding shareholders.

To Sum It Up

Should investors buy PPHE Hotel Group for the upcoming dividend? Earnings per share have been rising nicely although, even though its cashflow payout ratio is low, we question why PPHE Hotel Group is paying out so much of its profit. For Gilead, closing the US$7.8b Arcellx deal and moving ahead with the Tubulis acquisition point to a clear push to balance its HIV and antiviral base with higher risk, oncology-focused assets. Arcellx brings cell therapies that sit closer to Gilead’s Kite franchise, while Tubulis adds antibody drug conjugate technology that competes in the same broad arena as AstraZeneca, Daiichi Sankyo, Roche and others. Together, these moves concentrate more capital in areas where clinical execution, safety outcomes and manufacturing reliability often decide whether a program becomes a franchise or stays a niche product. Investors now have more of Gilead’s future tied to complex oncology platforms that can require substantial follow-on spending for trials, production facilities and commercial build out. On the other hand, management is addressing long term questions about how to supplement HIV cash flows, especially with policy and patent risks already on the radar. How quickly Arcellx and Tubulis assets progress through trials, and how they compete against therapies from companies such as Bristol Myers Squibb, Novartis and Merck, are likely to be closely watched as part of Gilead’s broader repositioning in cancer care.

How This Fits Into The Gilead Sciences Narrative

  • The Arcellx and Tubulis deals support the existing narrative that oncology and cell therapy can provide additional growth drivers alongside the HIV franchise as Gilead broadens its portfolio.
  • The size of the Arcellx acquisition and the added R&D commitment for Tubulis highlight execution and integration risks that already feature as concerns around pipeline diversification and market access.
  • The specific clinical profiles, timelines and potential competition for the acquired assets are not fully captured in the higher level focus on new launches and broader oncology expansion in the current narrative.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Large acquisitions and oncology build out add integration and clinical trial risk at a time when analysts already flag regulatory, patent and competitive pressures for the core HIV business.
  • ⚠️ A higher commitment to complex cell therapies and antibody drug conjugates could increase R&D and manufacturing costs, which may weigh on margins if key programs do not progress as planned.
  • 🎁 Bringing in Arcellx and Tubulis aligns with efforts to reduce reliance on legacy products, giving Gilead more potential sources of long term revenue from cancer treatments.
  • 🎁 The company’s strong cash generation and existing HIV and antiviral franchises provide financial capacity to support these acquisitions while continuing dividends and other capital priorities already highlighted by analysts.

What To Watch Going Forward

Investors may want to follow closing of the Arcellx tender offer, any updated guidance on deal related spending, and early commentary on how Arcellx and Tubulis will slot into Gilead’s existing oncology and cell therapy operations. Clinical milestones, safety updates, and competitive data from peers such as Bristol Myers Squibb, Novartis and Merck will help show whether these assets can stand out in crowded cancer indications. Management commentary on future business development, balance sheet flexibility given previous concerns about debt levels, and how HIV cash flows support oncology investment will also be important checkpoints.

data.title: Astronics Qi2 Wireless Charging Launch Tests Cabin Power Growth Story
data.description:
• Astronics (NasdaqGS:ATRO) has introduced its new EmPower Qi2 Wireless Charging Module for aircraft cabins, galleys, and crew rest areas.
  • The module supports the latest Qi enabled and MagSafe compatible devices and is designed for rapid maintenance and compliance with aviation standards.

Astronics focuses on aerospace and defense electronics, with cabin power and connectivity as a core part of its business. The EmPower Qi2 Wireless Charging Module fits directly into that franchise and aims to address airline and passenger demand for reliable onboard power as more devices rely on wireless charging.

For investors following NasdaqGS:ATRO, this product launch adds another building block to the company’s aircraft cabin systems portfolio. As airlines refresh cabins and look to standardize charging for a wider range of devices, adoption and feedback on this module may help indicate how Astronics’ technology roadmap aligns with customer priorities.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Astronics by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Astronics.

We've flagged 2 risks for Astronics. See which could impact your investment.

The EmPower Qi2 Wireless Charging Module looks like a logical extension of Astronics’ in-seat power footprint rather than a standalone bet. By supporting 25W charging, Qi2 and MagSafe compatibility, and integration into seats, galleys, and crew areas, it keeps Astronics in the conversation as airlines update cabins and as rivals like Safran, Collins Aerospace, and Thales also push passenger-experience hardware. Features such as backward compatibility with non-Qi2 devices, in-use status lights, and rapid module replacement in under three minutes are aimed at airlines’ two main concerns: aircraft downtime and maintenance complexity. Compliance with DO-160 and major aircraft original equipment manufacturer requirements, plus a 50,000 hour predicted mean time between failures, positions the product for use on a wide range of platforms if customers opt for it.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Astronics Narrative

  • The launch supports the existing narrative that Astronics is leaning into higher-margin cabin power and connectivity products that can benefit from aircraft production and retrofit cycles.
  • If the product requires meaningful upfront investment, or if adoption is slower than expected, it could temper some of the optimism around portfolio simplification and margin improvement.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on in-seat power leadership. Features like ultra-fast maintenance swaps and MagSafe-compatible alignment may not yet be fully reflected in expectations for recurring retrofit or aftermarket demand.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Astronics to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Astronics still carries a high level of debt, so product expansion like Qi2 needs to contribute to returns that justify the balance sheet risk.
  • ⚠️ Large one off items, such as past legal or restructuring charges, show that unexpected costs can affect reported results even when the product roadmap looks attractive.
  • 🎁 The company has been growing profit or revenue, and new cabin power products could support that trend if airlines and seat manufacturers adopt them.
  • 🎁 Analysts have flagged Astronics as trading below some fair value estimates, and a broader wireless charging offering may help support the view that the business has room to grow into that assessment.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how quickly the Qi2 Wireless Charging Module moves from product launch into real aircraft programs and retrofit orders, and whether airlines standardize on Astronics for both wired and wireless power. Watch for any commentary on pricing, margins, and certification progress with major aircraft manufacturers, as well as how the module features in discussions about backlog quality. Investors may also want to track competitive responses from other cabin-systems players and whether Astronics references Qi2 adoption in future updates on its in-seat power leadership.
